hints: Waiting for r successes is negative binomial.
E[flips for r heads] = r/p.
solution: The number of flips to get $3$ heads is $\mathrm{NegBinom}(3,\tfrac14)$ with mean $\dfrac{r}{p}=\dfrac{3}{1/4}=12.$

hints: Add the fill rates and subtract the drain rate.
Net rate = 1/20 + 1/10 − 1/15 = 5/60 = 1/12 pool per minute.
Time = 12 minutes.
solution: Net filling rate $=\tfrac1{20}+\tfrac1{10}-\tfrac1{15}=\tfrac{3+6-4}{60}=\tfrac{5}{60}=\tfrac1{12}$ pool per minute, so it takes $12$ minutes.

hints: Units digit in {1,3,7,9} means the product is odd and not a multiple of 5.
So every chosen number must be odd and not 5 ⇒ from {1,3,7,9}.
Each draw lands there w.p. 4/9 ⇒ (4/9)⁴ = 256/6561.
solution: A units digit of $1,3,7,9$ means the product is odd and not divisible by $5$. That requires every factor to be odd and not equal to $5$, i.e. drawn from $\{1,3,7,9\}$ (probability $\tfrac49$ each). So $P=\big(\tfrac49\big)^4=\dfrac{256}{6561}.$

hints: Imagine looking at a cube along its main diagonal.
Along the space diagonal the silhouette is a regular hexagon.
Maximum shadow has 6 sides.
solution: Orient the cube so the light points along a space (corner-to-corner) diagonal. The silhouette is then a regular hexagon, which is the most sides achievable. So $n=6.$

hints: Underlying X = product of two dice; the call pays max(X−19, 0). Compute its theoretical value.
E[max(X−19,0)] over 36 outcomes = 57/36 = 19/12.
A 2-wide market is centered at theo, so bid+ask = 2·theo = 19/6.
solution: The underlying is $X=$ product of two dice; the call pays $\max(X-19,0)$. Summing $(X-19)^+$ over the $36$ equally likely products gives $6+18+33=57$, so the theoretical value is $\dfrac{57}{36}=\dfrac{19}{12}$. A $2$-wide market is centered on theo, so $\text{bid}+\text{ask}=2\cdot\dfrac{19}{12}=\dfrac{19}{6}.$

hints: 8 decks = 416 cards; choose 3 from C(416,3). Count 3-rank runs, then remove straight flushes.
There are 12 three-rank runs (A23 … JQK and QKA). Each rank has 32 cards, so 32³ per run; subtract 4·8³ same-suit.
solution: With $8$ decks there are $416$ cards and $\binom{416}{3}=11{,}912{,}160$ ways to draw $3$. There are $12$ runs of three consecutive ranks (A23 through JQK, plus QKA). Each rank has $8\cdot4=32$ cards, so a run gives $32^3$ ordered-rank combos; subtract same-suit straight flushes $4\cdot8^3$. Per run: $32768-2048=30720$, times $12$ is $368{,}640$. Thus $P=\dfrac{368640}{11912160}=\dfrac{768}{24817}.$

hints: Write the two balances as equations in apple a, pear p, plum values.
p+3a=10·plum and a+6·plum=p ⇒ a=p−6plum.
Substitute: 4p=28·plum ⇒ p=7 plums.
solution: Let $a,p$ and a plum's weight be the variables. The conditions are $p+3a=10\,\text{plum}$ and $a+6\,\text{plum}=p$, so $a=p-6\,\text{plum}$. Substituting, $p+3(p-6\,\text{plum})=10\,\text{plum}\Rightarrow 4p=28\,\text{plum}\Rightarrow p=7$ plums.

hints: Sum is even iff both chosen primes are odd.
solution: The primes up to $20$ are $\{2,3,5,7,11,13,17,19\}$, of which only $2$ is even. The sum of two of them is even iff both are odd. So $P=\dfrac{\binom{7}{2}}{\binom{8}{2}}=\dfrac{21}{28}=\dfrac34.$

hints: List 2–10: primes {2,3,5,7} pay +n, composites {4,6,8,9,10} pay −n/2.
EV = −1/6 < 0, so pay nothing ⇒ 0.
solution:
Over $2$–$10$ (9 equally likely values), primes $2,3,5,7$ pay $+n$ and composites $4,6,8,9,10$ pay $-\tfrac n2$:
$$\mathbb{E}=\tfrac19\Big[(2+3+5+7)-\tfrac12(4+6+8+9+10)\Big]=\tfrac19\big(17-18.5\big)=-\tfrac16<0.$$
The game has negative value, so you should pay $\$0.$

hints: With replacement, P(jack)=1/13; you want a run of 4.
Expected draws for a run of k successes = (p^{−k}−1)/(1−p).
solution: Each draw is a jack with probability $p=\tfrac{4}{52}=\tfrac1{13}$. The expected number of draws to get a run of $k$ successes is $\dfrac{p^{-k}-1}{1-p}$. With $k=4$: $\dfrac{13^4-1}{1-\tfrac1{13}}=\dfrac{28560}{12/13}=28560\cdot\dfrac{13}{12}=30940.$

hints: X wins if the first hit happens on his turns: shots 1,4,7,… (≡1 mod 3).
solution:
X wins if the first successful shot occurs on one of his turns ($1,4,7,\dots$), i.e. after $3k$ misses then X hits:
$$P=\sum_{k\ge0}\Big(\tfrac12\Big)^{3k}\cdot\tfrac12=\frac{1/2}{1-1/8}=\frac{4}{7}.$$

hints: Common attainable values are 1–6 (die limits this).
For a fixed v∈{1..6}: P(die)=1/6, P(roulette)=1/38, P(card)=4/52=1/13.
solution:
All three can simultaneously show only the values $1$–$6$ (limited by the die). For a fixed such value: $P(\text{die})=\tfrac16$, $P(\text{roulette})=\tfrac1{38}$, $P(\text{card})=\tfrac{4}{52}=\tfrac1{13}$. Summing over the $6$ values,
$$P=6\cdot\tfrac16\cdot\tfrac1{38}\cdot\tfrac1{13}=\tfrac{1}{494}.$$
